programs.name: Medicaid for the Elderly and People with Disabilities (MEPD)
programs.what_it_does: Provides medical coverage including long-term care services for eligible seniors and disabled individuals. In Texas, it is administered through programs like STAR+PLUS for managed care support to help seniors stay in the community.[1][5]
programs.who_its_for: Seniors aged 65+ or disabled individuals with low income and resources, meeting specific Medicaid eligibility criteria.
programs.benefit_value: Varies by eligibility; covers premiums, deductibles, copays, and long-term services.

programs.name: Community Care for the Aged and Disabled (CCAD) Waivers
programs.what_it_does: Offers home and community-based services through waivers like Community Attendant Services (CAS) to prevent nursing home placement. Provides personal care, respite, and other supports for independent living.[5]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors 60+ or disabled adults at risk of institutionalization.
programs.benefit_value: Varies; includes in-home attendant services, respite care, and minor home modifications based on need.

programs.name: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)
programs.what_it_does: Delivers comprehensive medical and social services to eligible seniors in community settings, including adult day health and transportation. Coordinates all Medicare and Medicaid-covered care through PACE centers in Texas.[6]
programs.who_its_for: Seniors 55+ certified as nursing facility eligible, able to live safely in the community.
programs.benefit_value: Full coverage of approved services with no copays for eligible participants.

programs.name: Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI, QDWI)
programs.what_it_does: Pays Medicare premiums, deductibles, and copayments for low-income beneficiaries. QMB covers Parts A & B fully, SLMB and QI cover Part B premiums, QDWI covers Part A for working disabled.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Medicare enrollees with income below program limits (e.g., QMB: $1,275 individual/$1,724 couple monthly) and resources under $9,430 individual/$14,130 couple.
programs.benefit_value: Premium payments up to $185/month Part B (2026 est.); full cost-sharing for QMB.

programs.name: Texas Simplified Application Project (TSAP) for SNAP
programs.what_it_does: Provides food assistance benefits via EBT card with simplified application, no renewal interview, and longer certification for seniors. Helps purchase nutritious food to combat hunger.[1][4]
programs.who_its_for: Seniors 60+ with income/resources meeting limits (resources $4,500), often SSI recipients.
programs.benefit_value: Average $188/month per person (2025); up to max benefit based on household size.

programs.name: Low-Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)
programs.what_it_does: Offers help with energy bills, crisis assistance, and weatherization for low-income households. Administered in Texas for heating/cooling needs.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors (below 150% FPL), SSI/SNAP/Veterans benefits recipients.
programs.benefit_value: One-time grants based on available federal funding; average $500-$1,000 per household.

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance Program (WAP)
programs.what_it_does: Provides home energy efficiency improvements like insulation and sealing to reduce utility costs. Available through Texas LIHEAP channels for seniors.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Income below 150% FPL, including seniors on SSI/SNAP/Veterans benefits.
programs.benefit_value: Free home upgrades valued at $3,000-$7,000 on average.

programs.name: Health Insurance Counseling and Advocacy Program (HICAP/SHIP)
programs.what_it_does: Offers free counseling on Medicare, Medigap, and savings programs. Helps Texas seniors navigate options and appeal denials.[1]
programs.who_its_for: All Medicare beneficiaries, especially low-income seniors.
programs.benefit_value: Free one-on-one counseling; potential savings of thousands on premiums/copays.

programs.name: Meals on Wheels (via Texas Area Agencies on Aging)
programs.what_it_does: Delivers nutritious meals to homebound seniors and offers congregate meals at centers. Supports nutrition and social well-being.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Homebound seniors 60+ unable to prepare meals, low-income prioritized.
programs.benefit_value: 5-14 meals/week; $3 suggested donation, free for many.

programs.name: Family Caregiver Support Program
programs.what_it_does: Provides respite care, caregiver training, and support services to relieve family caregivers. Funded through HHS for Texas seniors.[2][4]
programs.who_its_for: Family caregivers of seniors 60+ or disabled adults, low-income.
programs.benefit_value: Respite grants up to $5,000/year in some areas; training and counseling.

programs.name: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)
programs.what_it_does: Offers part-time paid training jobs in community service to unemployed low-income seniors. Helps transition to unsubsidized employment.[6]
programs.who_its_for: Unemployed seniors 55+ with income at or below 125% FPL.
programs.benefit_value: Minimum wage pay at 20 hours/week; job placement support.

programs.name: Legal Aid for Seniors (via Area Agencies on Aging/Legal Hotlines)
programs.what_it_does: Provides free legal assistance for issues like benefits, housing, and abuse. Texas seniors access via 2-1-1 or HHS programs.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors 60+ facing legal barriers to services.
programs.benefit_value: Free consultations and representation; resolves benefits claims.

programs.name: Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program
programs.what_it_does: Advocates for residents in long-term care facilities, investigating complaints and ensuring quality care. Operates statewide in Texas.[6]
programs.who_its_for: Residents of nursing homes, assisted living, and similar facilities.
programs.benefit_value: Free advocacy services; resolves 80%+ of complaints.
